name: Grand staircase
description: Wide ceremonial staircase that connects two storeys while acting as the focal element of a great hall or entry vestibule. Unlike a utilitarian stair, the grand staircase is dimensioned for procession: a single broad flight five to eight blocks wide, or twin curved flights that diverge at floor level and meet again at an upper balcony landing. A continuous balustrade with carved newel posts frames each tread line, a central runner of carpet or coloured stone marks the ceremonial axis, and the half-landing is treated as a small stage facing the room below. The staircase is always placed so that whoever enters the hall sees it head-on, making it the visual climax of the ground-floor circulation as in Renaissance palaces, Victorian mansions, and opera-house lobbies.
placement_rules: Place the staircase on the principal axis of the great hall or entry vestibule so its lower step faces the main entrance directly; a grand staircase that is off-axis loses its focal role.
Allow at least three blocks of clear floor in front of the bottom step so the stair reads as a stage entered from the room rather than from a corridor.
Single-flight version: one continuous run 5-8 blocks wide rising the full storey height, with a half-landing at the midpoint widened by one block on each side.
Twin-curved version: two symmetric flights branch outward from a shared lower platform, curve along the side walls, and rejoin at a central upper balcony that overlooks the hall.
Cap every newel — the post at the foot and head of each flight, plus the half-landing — with a distinct block (lantern, decorated head, or carved stone) so the balustrade is visually punctuated.
Run the central runner unbroken from the bottom step up to the balcony; it must be one block narrower than the flight so the stair edges remain visible.
Reserve the wall behind the upper balcony for the largest window, painting, or coat-of-arms in the building — the staircase frames whatever is centred there.
lighting_ventilation: A grand staircase is lit ceremonially from above: a large window, oculus, or chandelier hangs centred over the half-landing so anyone ascending walks into the light. Newel-post lanterns or wall sconces flanking each flight provide secondary illumination at tread level. Because the staircase opens fully into the double-height hall it shares that volume's ventilation; no dedicated openings are required on the stair itself.

renaissance.notes: Symmetric twin-curved flights in white quartz with pillar balustrades, sea-lantern newels, and a red carpet runner — the staircase of a Florentine palazzo opening onto a salone.

victorian.notes: Heavy dark-oak single flight with turned-fence balustrade, brass-style lanterns on every newel, and a deep red runner — the front hall of a Victorian townhouse.

gothic.notes: Stone-brick single flight with cobblestone-wall balustrade and torch-topped newels rising to a chiseled-stone landing — the processional stair of a cathedral chapter house or castle keep.
